## 1. Introduction

[Urolithiasis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/urolithiasis) is a highly prevalent condition with significant burden on the healthcare system worldwide. The annual incidence of urolithiasis is 0.5% and the lifetime risk of developing urolithiasis is about 10%–15% in the Western world but it can be as high as 20% to 25% in the middle east countries [[1]](#bib1). Collaborative research is essential to address the treatment and prevention of urolithiasis. The majority of [kidney stones](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrolithiasis) are asymptomatic at the time of presentation except for acute surgical conditions. Patients with asymptomatic renal calculi often look for alternative medicines. It widely accepted that [medicinal plants](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/medicinal-plant) have played a significant role in various ancient traditional systems of medicine. There is sufficient evidence that herbal extracts have anti-urolithiatic potential. Therapeutic potential of these herbs is also studied by *in vitro* and *in vivo* studies [[2]](#bib2).

The increasing use of traditional therapies demands scientifically sound evidence for principles behind these therapies and for the effectiveness of such medicines [[3]](#bib3). Such remedies can be validated at a global level by ‘reverse pharmacology’ approach. In the concept of [reverse pharmacology](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/reverse-pharmacology), safety remains the most important starting point while efficacy becomes a matter of validation [[4]](#bib4). Hence, considering the present scenario, we developed the investigational product (IP), an herbal formulation Subap Plus capsules (IP), under our research program. This formulation was prepared from the extracts of *Crateva nurvala* Buch-ham, *[Musa](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/musa "Learn more about Musa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")* *x paradisiaca* Linn, *[Achyranthes aspera](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/achyranthes-aspera "Learn more about Achyranthes aspera from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")* Linn and *Hordeum vulgare* Linn. All the herbs used in the preparation of Subap Plus capsules are mentioned in Indian traditional system of medicine (Ayurved), [[5]](#bib5), [[6]](#bib6), [[7]](#bib7), [[8]](#bib8) (<https://nccih.nih.gov/research/policies/naturalproduct.htm>). In the present study, the safety and efficacy of this formulation were evaluated in patients with asymptomatic renal calculi (4–9 mm). The present study was based on our earlier experience of herbal formulation in the management of urolithiasis [[9]](#bib9).

## 2. Materials and methods

The materials selected from the Ayurvedic literature have the properties like anti-urolithiatic

* (*Lekhan* and *Bhedan* of stone material), [Diuretic](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/diuretic-agent) (*Mutral*), [Antispasmodic](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/spasmolytic-agent) (*Vedanashamak*) and Anti-inflammatory (*Shothagnah*).

### 2.1. Manufacture of investigational product(IP)

1. Dried stem bark of *C**.* *nurvala* Buch-ham (Three leaved caper/*Varun*), 2. Stem and roots of *[Musa](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/musa "Learn more about Musa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")* *x paradisiaca* Linn (Banana/*Kadali*), 3. Whole plant of *A**.* *aspera* Linn (Chaff flower/*Apamarg*) and 4. Seeds of *H**.* *vulgare* Linn (Barley/*Yav*).

The authentication of the raw materials was done at Agharkar research institute, (Department of Science and Technology, GOI) Pune. Voucher specimens of each plant/parts 1). *C. nurvala*- Voucher no.-04/09, 2). *M. x Paradasiaca*- Voucher no. 03/06, 3). *A aspera,* Voucher no.*-*30/05 and 4). *H. Vulgare,* Voucher no. −16/06/09) has been retained in the research laboratory.

### 2.2. Method of preparation

*C. Nurvala*- Preparation of *Varun swaras bhavit Varun churna Varun Bharad* prepared from pulverization of dried stem bark of *Varun*wasmixed with water and boiled (*quath*/ *kadha*/ water decoction). This *quath* was added to *Varun bharad* and kneaded to obtain a mixture of homogenized powder. The product obtained was dried in an oven. This is called *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna*. *Kadali swaras bhavit Varun*- Stems of *M. x Paradasiaca* were chopped into pieces and grounded in a mixer to obtain an aqueous extract, which was added to *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna*. This was further grounded to make a homogenous composition which was dried in oven to obtain a fine powder. This is called *K**.* *swaras bhavit Varun*. Hence, *K**.* *swaras* was used as *bhavna drvaya* for *Varunchurna* (There are no other terms known to us for *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna* and *K**.* *swaras bhavit Varun* that could be cited in traditional literature). While imparting ‘*bhavana*’ the drug is triturated with *swaras* till it gets dried. We have dried it in oven and agree that it should not be done likewise.

*M. x Paradasiaca*- Banana stems, roots and *kand* were used to prepare *Kadali Kshar* as per herbal guidelines [[10]](#bib10).

*Acharynthes Aspera*- Whole plant of *Apamarg* consisting of flowers,leaves, seeds, roots and fruits (*panchang*) were used to obtain *Apamarg kshar* as per herbal guidelines [[10]](#bib10).

*Hordeum Vulgare*- *Yav* grains are used to prepare *Yavkshar* as per herbal guidelines [[10]](#bib10).

Final formulation composition of our formulation was: Each 500 mg capsule contains–*V.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna* (250 mg), *Kadali Kshar* (75 mg), *Yav kshar* (100 mg) and *Apamarg Kshar* (75 mg). (Dosage- One capule- Twice daily, after meals).

Placebo- Identically sized and colored capsules filled with lactose were used in the same dosage so as to match the active ingredient capsule. All this material was manufactured at a GMP (Good manufacturing Practicces) certified facility and was tested in National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories (NABL) accredited laboratory for [heavy metal](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/heavy-metal) contamination and microbial count.

### 2.3. Tests involved in standardization process-

The following tests were performed as per herbal pharmacopeia viz. [Foreign matter](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/foreign-body); Organoleptic study – appearance, color, taste and fracture; Macroscopic and microscopic studies; Powder analysis –Ash and extractives and TLC in an accredited laboratory. Laboratory persons were blinded to identify the extract and control capsules. The samples and all the standardization reports of the intermediate and final product have been retained at the manufacturing facility. The staff involved in the study was trained in the evidence based herbal medicine interventions.

The pre-clinical acute and sub-acute safety of this formulation were established in rodents using the Organization of Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines respectively. The acute LD 50 cut off for IP was found to be > 2000 mg/kg in [Swiss albino mice](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/swiss-webster-mouse) and NOAEL was found to be more than 450 mg/kg by oral route for 90 days in [Wistar rats](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/wistar-rat) [[11]](#bib11). The efficacy of IP was tested and was confirmed in [Ethylene Glycol](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/ethylene-glycol) induced rat model (unpublished data).

### 2.4. Methods

This was a prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial conducted between September 2010 and December 2013. The trial was registered with [Clinical Trial](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/clinical-trial) Registry of India with registration number: CTRI/2009/091/000946. The study protocol was approved from Institutional Ethics Committee (IEC), at Ace Hospital and Research Centre, Pune, India. The study was conducted in accordance with the principles that have their origin in the Declaration of Helsinki. Each study participant provided written informed consent before participating in the study. The principal investigator is qualified in both Ayurvedic and allopathic systems of medicine with more than 30 years of experience of and is licensed in India. Both, the principal investigator and enrolled patients were kept blinded about the intervention.

Patients who were asymptomatic at the time of the first visit were screened. Eligible and willing patients were enrolled from the outpatient Department of Urology at Ace Hospital, Pune. Patients visiting the outpatient department with either ultrasound or KUB X-ray (abdomen) reports were also screened for eligibility. In both the cases, those with a [renal stone](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrolithiasis) size between 4 mm and 9 mm, underwent non-contrast computerized tomography (CT) scan for confirmation.

Patients with complications and requiring surgical interventions were excluded. Patients with high values of serum. creatinine, serum [glutamic pyruvic transaminase](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/alanine-aminotransferase) (SGPT), serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase (SGOT), [alkaline phosphatase](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/alkaline-phosphatase) (ALP) and [HbA1c](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/hemoglobin-a1c) at baseline were also excluded.

Eligible patients were randomized (1:1; block randomization) to one of the treatment group to receive herbal formulation (treatment group) or placebo (placebo group). The randomization, enrollment, assignment and dispensing (medicine/placebo)was done by independent healthcare professionals not involved with the trial. The assessment too was done by them. Enrolled patients received one capsule of 500 mg (either herbal formulation or placebo) twice-a-day after meals for six months. Dosage was determined from our earlier trial of HERBMED and the dosage mentioned in the traditional Ayurvedic literature as per the nature of the formulation. Heavy metal tests were carried out as per NABL laboratory standards for four metals viz Lead, Cadmium, Mercury and Arsenic. The methods employed for testing these metals were- (AAS) SOP 025 (Pb), SOP 028(Cd), SOP 027(Hg), and SOP 026(As). The concentration of these metals was within acceptable limits.

Qualitative tests were conducted at Indian Drugs Research Association and Laboratory (IDRA and L), Pune. Certificates of analysis have been issued by the laboratory. Samples of *Kadali*, *Yav* and *A**.* *kshar* were analyzed for physical features (color, consistency) loss on drying (in%), acid insoluble ash (in %) and PH(in 10% aqueous solution). The *kshars* were also assayed for-sodium, potassium and iron (in %).

The products obtained from raw herbal materials/extracts, i. e, *V**.* *swaras bhavit Varun churna*and the final formulation (Subap Plus) were analyzed for physical features that included water extractive (in %), alcohol extractive (in %), bulk density (in %) and the particle size too (determined using sieves of varying sizes) apart from the analysis of sodium, potassium and iron (in %).

* Extraction-Methanol,
* Adsorbent -Silica gel G6OF254,
* Solvent system-n-Butenol:Acetic acid:water (in 6:1:2),

Detection–UV 254 nm, UV 365 nm, and [Anisaldehyde](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/anisaldehyde) [Sulfuric acid](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/sulfuric-acid) Reagent.

The samples and all the standardization reports of the intermediate and final product have been retained at the manufacturing facility.

Blood sample for biochemical evaluation of complete blood count, blood sugar levels, serum creatinine, [blood urea](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/ureaplasma) nitrogen, serum electrolytes, serum [bilirubin](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/bilirubin) (total), SGPT, SGOT, ALP, total proteins, HbA1c and urine (routine and microscopic) was performed at baseline and at the end of the study. Follow-up was done every month for pain and expulsion of calculi if any. No rescue medicine was provided during follow-up. Patient was asked to take tablet diclofenac sodium 50 mg (as necessary or twice-daily) in case of severe pain. The CT scan was done at the start of the study and also when the patient provided the history of expulsion or at the end of the study.

The clinical assessment for pain was analyzed by visual analog scale (VAS), on a scale of 0–10. Expulsion and change in the size and density of calculi were assessed by CT scan. Size of calculi was calculated by the surface area of the stone on the basis of length and width (Guidelines on [Urolithiasis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/urolithiasis): European Association of Urology, 2001). In case of multiple calculi, the cumulative diameter (mean value of multiple stones) was considered for calculation of surface area. Density was calculated by considering the largest length and width intersection point to avoid error. Density was measured in Hounsfield unit threshold by CT scan.

# Original Research Article (Clinical) Safety and efficacy of an herbal formulation in patients with renal calculi - A 28 week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel group study

Author links open overlay panelSuresh B. Patankar a b, A.M. Mujumdar a, Fanthome Bernard a, Phadke Supriya a

## Abstract

### Background

[Urolithiasis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/urolithiasis) is a growing problem worldwide. Many a times, asymptomatic stones are kept under observation. Many [herbal preparations](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/herbaceous-agent) are available for the same, but they lack proper scientific documentation.

### Objective

To study the anti-urolithiatic effect of an herbal preparation, Subap Plus (IP) capsules in patients with asymptomatic [renal calculi](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrolithiasis) of size ranging from 4 to 9 mm.

### Material and methods

This was a prospective, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial conducted in a tertiary care hospital in Pune, India.

Patients with asymptomatic renal calculi of 4–9 mm size were randomized (1:1, block randomization) to one of the group Subap Plus (treatment group) or placebo (placebo group). The study outcome included change in visual analog scale (VAS), change in the surface area and density of calculi and their expulsion. Statistical analysis was performed using student's t-test and Chi-square test.

### Results

A total of 120 patients were screened and 84 were enrolled who met the eligibility criteria, of which 65 patients completed the trial (treatment, n = 34; placebo, n = 31). The VAS score significantly decreased in the treatment group (6.9–1.8) than placebo group (7.2–6.8) (p < 0.001). The surface area and density were decreased by 47.58% (p < 0.008) and 43.01% (p < 0.001), respectively, in the treatment group than the placebo group. The expulsion of calculi was significantly higher in the treatment group than placebo group (20.59 vs. 3.23%, p < 0.03).

### Conclusion

Patients treated with herbal formulation showed better expulsion rate and reduction in surface area and density than the placebo group.

## Keywords

Urolithiasis

Kidney stone

Calculus

Herbal formulation

## 1. Introduction

[Urolithiasis](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/urolithiasis) is a highly prevalent condition with significant burden on the healthcare system worldwide. The annual incidence of urolithiasis is 0.5% and the lifetime risk of developing urolithiasis is about 10%–15% in the Western world but it can be as high as 20% to 25% in the middle east countries [[1]](#bib1). Collaborative research is essential to address the treatment and prevention of urolithiasis. The majority of [kidney stones](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/nephrolithiasis) are asymptomatic at the time of presentation except for acute surgical conditions. Patients with asymptomatic renal calculi often look for alternative medicines. It widely accepted that [medicinal plants](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/medicinal-plant) have played a significant role in various ancient traditional systems of medicine. There is sufficient evidence that herbal extracts have anti-urolithiatic potential. Therapeutic potential of these herbs is also studied by *in vitro* and *in vivo* studies [[2]](#bib2).

The increasing use of traditional therapies demands scientifically sound evidence for principles behind these therapies and for the effectiveness of such medicines [[3]](#bib3). Such remedies can be validated at a global level by ‘reverse pharmacology’ approach. In the concept of [reverse pharmacology](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/reverse-pharmacology), safety remains the most important starting point while efficacy becomes a matter of validation [[4]](#bib4). Hence, considering the present scenario, we developed the investigational product (IP), an herbal formulation Subap Plus capsules (IP), under our research program. This formulation was prepared from the extracts of *Crateva nurvala* Buch-ham, *[Musa](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/musa "Learn more about Musa from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")* *x paradisiaca* Linn, *[Achyranthes aspera](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/achyranthes-aspera "Learn more about Achyranthes aspera from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")* Linn and *Hordeum vulgare* Linn. All the herbs used in the preparation of Subap Plus capsules are mentioned in Indian traditional system of medicine (Ayurved), [[5]](#bib5), [[6]](#bib6), [[7]](#bib7), [[8]](#bib8) (<https://nccih.nih.gov/research/policies/naturalproduct.htm>). In the present study, the safety and efficacy of this formulation were evaluated in patients with asymptomatic renal calculi (4–9 mm). The present study was based on our earlier experience of herbal formulation in the management of urolithiasis [[9]](#bib9).

## 2. Materials and methods

The materials selected from the Ayurvedic literature have the properties like anti-urolithiatic

* (*Lekhan* and *Bhedan* of stone material), [Diuretic](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/diuretic-agent) (*Mutral*), [Antispasmodic](/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/spasmolytic-agent) (*Vedanashamak*) and Anti-inflammatory (*Shothagnah*).

### 2.1. Manufacture of investigational product(IP)

Whole plant of *A**.* *aspera* Linn (Chaff flower/*Apamarg*) and 4. Seeds of *H**.* *vulgare* Linn (Barley/*Yav*).

The authentication of the raw materials was done at Agharkar research institute, (Department of Science and Technology, GOI) Pune. Voucher specimens of each plant/parts 1). *C. nurvala*- Voucher no.-04/09, 2). *M. x Paradasiaca*- Voucher no. 03/06, 3). *A aspera,* Voucher no.*-*30/05 and 4). *H. Vulgare,* Voucher no. −16/06/09) has been retained in the research laboratory.

### 2.2. Method of preparation

*C. Nurvala*- Preparation of *Varun swaras bhavit Varun churna Varun Bharad* prepared from pulverization of dried stem bark of *Varun*wasmixed with water and boiled (*quath*/ *kadha*/ water decoction). This *quath* was added to *Varun bharad* and kneaded to obtain a mixture of homogenized powder. The product obtained was dried in an oven. This is called *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna*. *Kadali swaras bhavit Varun*- Stems of *M. x Paradasiaca* were chopped into pieces and grounded in a mixer to obtain an aqueous extract, which was added to *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna*. This was further grounded to make a homogenous composition which was dried in oven to obtain a fine powder. This is called *K**.* *swaras bhavit Varun*. Hence, *K**.* *swaras* was used as *bhavna drvaya* for *Varunchurna* (There are no other terms known to us for *V**.* *swaras bhavit V**.* *churna* and *K**.* *swaras bhavit Varun* that could be cited in traditional literature). While imparting ‘*bhavana*’ the drug is triturated with *swaras* till it gets dried. We have dried it in oven and agree that it should not be done likewise.

*Acharynthes Aspera*- Whole plant of *Apamarg* consisting of flowers,leaves, seeds, roots and fruits (*panchang*) were used to obtain *Apamarg kshar* as per herbal guidelines [[10]](#bib10).

*Hordeum Vulgare*- *Yav* grains are used to prepare *Yavkshar* as per herbal guidelines [[10]](#bib10).

takeaways.text:
- The article describes a 28-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial evaluating a herbal formulation for renal calculi.
- The investigational product Subap Plus contains *Achyranthes aspera* Linn along with *Crataeva Nurvala*, *Musa paradisiaca*, and *Hordeum Vulgare*.
- Patients enrolled in the trial had asymptomatic renal calculi ranging in size from 4 to 9 mm.
- Extract of *Achyranthes aspera* has shown effective inhibitory action on CaOx nucleation and growth in vitro.
- The study results reported a significant reduction in the surface area and density of stones in the treatment group compared to the placebo group.
- A total of 84 patients were randomized in the study, with 34 in the treatment group and 31 in the placebo group completing the trial.
- The herbal formulation is proposed to act on the phases of stone formation including nucleation, growth, aggregation, and crystal retention. <image:4954> <image:4953> <image:4955> <image:4953>

# FDA warns about heavy metal poisoning associated with certain unapproved ayurvedic drug products

* [Fraudulent Products](/drugs/medication-health-fraud/fraudulent-products)

 + [Bodybuilding Products: SARMs Cause Harm](/drugs/fraudulent-products/certain-bodybuilding-products-put-consumers-risk-heart-attack-stroke-serious-liver-damage-and-more)
 + [Heavy Metal Poisoning Associated with Ayurvedic Drug Products](/drugs/fraudulent-products/fda-warns-about-heavy-metal-poisoning-associated-certain-unapproved-ayurvedic-drug-products)

FDA warns consumers that using unapproved ayurvedic products containing harmful levels of heavy metals may cause [heavy metal poisoning](https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/heavy-metal-poisoning/)[External Link Disclaimer](http://www.fda.gov/about-fda/website-policies/website-disclaimer). Using these products may result in high blood pressure, kidney injury, fatigue, gastrointestinal distress and neurologic symptoms. There are no FDA-approved ayurvedic products. Ayurvedic products marketed to prevent, treat, diagnose or cure any disease are illegally marketed in the U.S.

[Ayurvedic medicine](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/ayurvedic-medicine-in-depth) is a traditional system of healing arts that originated in India. Ayurvedic products may include ingredients such as spices, herbs, vitamins, proteins and minerals, and some include heavy metals, such as lead, mercury, arsenic, iron and zinc. However, these products typically are not labeled to contain heavy metals such as lead, mercury or arsenic.

## FDA’s recent product testing

![rheumacare image](/files/rheumacare%20image.jpg "rheumacare image")

An FDA laboratory recently tested Rheumacare Ayurvedic Proprietary Medicine purchased from NavaFresh.com. The testing found high levels of lead and mercury, as well as the presence of strychnine, brucine toxins and arsenic.

The agency urges consumers not to use Rheumacare. Consumers who have this product should discard it. The agency testing was based on an adverse event report of a consumer who used the product and experienced extremely elevated blood lead levels.

## Symptoms of heavy metal poisoning or toxicity

Chronic exposure to heavy metals can lead to their accumulation in the body and cause harmful health effects like:

* high blood pressure
* kidney injury
* fatigue
* gastrointestinal symptoms such as abdominal pain, constipation, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting
* nervous system-related symptoms such as burning or tingling in the fingers and/or toes, weakness in limbs, dizziness or a spinning sensation, feeling agitated and not thinking clearly or being confused

## Risks of heavy metal poisoning

* Adults and children can become [very sick when heavy metals accumulate in their bodies](https://www.cdc.gov/niosh/topics/repro/heavymetals.html) over time, and large acute ingestions over short periods of time can be life-threatening.
* Chronic exposures to arsenic may increase the risk of cancer.
* If a mother has heavy metals in her body, the metals may enter her breast milk and be passed onto the baby during breastfeeding.

## **Information for consumers**

* Talk with your health care provider before using ayurvedic products.
* Talk to your health care provider or [consult with a poison center](https://www.poison.org/)[External Link Disclaimer](http://www.fda.gov/about-fda/website-policies/website-disclaimer) (1-800-222-1222) if you have used ayurvedic products and you are experiencing symptoms of possible [heavy metal poisoning](#symptoms).
* Report products suspected of containing heavy metals to [FDA’s MedWatch program](/safety/medwatch-fda-safety-information-and-adverse-event-reporting-program).

## Information for health care providers

* Ask patients about their use of all products, including ayurvedic products.
* Consider the potential for heavy metal toxicity among patients who indicate they use ayurvedic products if they exhibit signs or symptoms of heavy metal toxicity.
* Warn patients about the risks of heavy metal poisoning from ayurvedic products.
* Report products suspected of containing heavy metals to [FDA’s MedWatch program](/safety/medwatch-fda-safety-information-and-adverse-event-reporting-program).

## Additional FDA actions to protect consumers

FDA works to protect consumers from potentially dangerous products by:

* Reviewing adverse event reports of heavy metal poisoning associated with ayurvedic products and tests certain products for heavy metals. When FDA identifies products with heavy metals, the agency recommends the company recall the product to protect consumers.
* Issuing [warning letters](/inspections-compliance-enforcement-and-criminal-investigations/compliance-actions-and-activities/warning-letters) to manufacturers of these products and adds companies to import alert to help stop these products from entering the U.S market ([import alert 66-41](https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cms_ia/importalert_190.html) or [import alert 99-42](https://cms.fda.gov/vts/imports_publish/private/importalert_1167.html)) to protect consumers.

retrievals.retrieved_content:
**Instagram Video**
Author: @PRARBDHA AYURVED(vd Siddharth Kumar)
Posted: 2025-09-18
Duration: 12.25s
Views: 0 - Likes: 15 - Comments: 0

The apamarga plant (Achyranthes aspera) offers numerous medicinal benefits in Ayurveda, including anti-inflammatory, digestive, and diuretic properties. It is traditionally used to treat respiratory issues like asthma and coughs, skin ailments, and kidney stones, and can also aid in wound healing and relieve digestive discomfort. The plant is available in various forms, such as paste, oil, and powder, but should only be used under medical supervision, especially for conditions like kidney stones or in pregnant women. 
Key Health Benefits of Apamarga
Digestive Health: Apamarga stimulates digestive enzymes and can help regulate bowel movements, relieving indigestion and diarrhea. 
Respiratory Support: Its expectorant properties help clear mucus, making it useful for coughs, asthma, and bronchitis. 
Skin and Wound Care: With its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, apamarga can be applied as a paste to heal wounds, ulcers, and skin infections like eczema and psoriasis. 
Urinary Health: As a natural diuretic, it helps flush toxins from the kidneys and may assist in breaking down kidney stones. 
Anti-inflammatory Effects: It can reduce joint and muscle pain by alleviating inflammation. 
Worm Infestation: It has an anti-worm property that helps expel intestinal worms. 
Anti-diabetic Properties: Some research suggests apamarga can help regulate blood sugar levels. 
How It's Used
Powder/Paste: Used topically for skin issues and wounds, or internally for digestive problems. 
Juice/Decoction: The juice of fresh leaves can be used for earaches and mouth ulcers, while a decoction can be made for kidney stones. 
Apamarga Kshara: An alkaline form used for conditions like acid-related stomach issues and for preparing surgical threads for anal fistula. 
Precautions
Medical Supervision: It is crucial to consult with a healthcare professional before using apamarga, as dosage and application vary by condition. 
Pregnancy: The plant is generally not recommended for pregnant or nursing women. 
Allergies: Individuals with allergies to plants in the Amaranthaceae family should avoid it.

# Chirchita (Apamarg/Prickly Chaff): Ayurvedic Benefits, Uses, and Side Effects

Jan 27, 2025

0 comments
 <image:4957> 
## What is Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)?

Prickly Chaff Flower, known as Chirchita (Achyranthes aspera) is an Ayurvedic medicinal herbs having plethora of health benefits. So it grows in tropical and sub-tropical areas, and has extremely powerful healing properties. Chirchita is known for supporting digestion, detoxifying the body, and promoting overall well-being.

It is mainly used for digestive health, as it alleviates constipation, indigestion, and bloating by improving gut function. It is a natural blood purifier as well, helping the body eliminate toxins and improve skin quality. However, the Antioxidants in Chirchita make it very good for joints and muscles as it reduces swelling and pain, thus helpful in Arthritis and Relieved when used on joints. 

### Synonyms: Apamarga, Prickly Chaff, and Regional Names

Chirchita has different names in various cultures and languages. It is known as Apamarga in Sanskrit and Chirchita in Hindi. Also, it is also known as Devil's Horsewhip in English.

### Botanical Information and Classification

* - Scientific Name: Achyranthes aspera
* - Family: Amaranthaceae
* - Genus: Achyranthes

## Chirchita Plant Overview

Chirchita is an erect, woody herb that grows up to 1–2 meters in height. It has small greenish flowers, rough stems, and characteristic spiny seeds that stick to clothing and animal fur.

### Habitat: Where It Grows Naturally

It grows abundantly in tropical and subtropical areas. Also, it can be commonly found in India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, and parts of Africa. It is found to thrive in waste places, roadsides, and cultivated lands.

Ayurveda uses all the parts of the Chirchita plant, including:

* - Leaves
* - Roots
* - Seeds
* - Stem

## Ayurvedic Significance of Chirchita

One of the most medicinal plants mentioned in the Charaka Saṁhitā and Sushruda Saṁhitā is chirchita, which can bring genuine healing to a number of conditions in human beings. It has numerous healing properties which have proven to be beneficial since a long time. 

Dosha Balancing Properties of Chirchita: Vata, Pitta, and Kapha

* - Balances Vata and Kapha doshas
* - Helps in Pitta disorders when used in moderation

## Chirchita Plant Benefits

* - Anti-inflammatory
* - Diuretic
* - Digestive stimulant
* - Blood purifier
* - Antimicrobial

## Health Benefits of Chirchita

#### 1. Digestive Health
 <image:4958> 
Chirchita is also known to digest the food easily as it induces appetite, prevents bloating, and treats constipation.

#### 2. Skin Care

Since Chirchita contains antimicrobial properties, it is also used in the treatment of infections on the skin, wounds, and acne.

#### 3. Kidney and Urinary Tract Health

Apamarga is used as a diuretic to cleanse the kidneys and avoid infection of the urinary tract.

#### 4. Blood Purification

It purifies the blood and cleanses the skin by removing the toxins.

#### 5. Role in Weight Management

Chirchita increases the metabolism and facilitates the loss of fats, hence useful in managing weight.

### Other Therapeutic Uses

* - Relieves joint pain and inflammation
* - Treats respiratory issues like asthma and cough

## How to Use Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)

### Forms: Powder, Juice, Decoction, and Oil

* - Powder: Used for digestion and detoxification
* - Juice: Beneficial for skin and kidney health
* - Decoction: Helps with respiratory and urinary issues
* - Oil: Applied externally for wounds and joint pain

### Recipes: DIY Remedies Using Chirchita

1. - For Digestive Health: Mix 1 tsp Chirchita powder with warm water and honey.
2. - For Skin Issues: Apply Chirchita paste on acne and wounds.
3. - For Kidney Health: Drink Chirchita decoction once daily.

### Dosage Recommendations

* - Powder: 1–3 grams per day
* - Juice: 10–20 ml per day
* - Decoction: 30–50 ml per day

## Apamarga Plant Benefits in Ayurveda

The anti-inflammatory properties help to lessen swelling and pain.

### Benefits for Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
 <image:4956> 
Chirchita helps regulate blood sugar levels, making it useful for diabetics.Chirchita helps in curing the metabolic disorders and regulating blood sugar levels, Thus it is considered useful for the purpose of diabetes.

### Wound Healing and Antimicrobial Properties

It Improve wound healing and protects against bacterial infection. Side Effects and Precautions

## Side Effects and Precautions

### Who Should Avoid Chirchita?

* - Pregnant and breastfeeding women
* - Individuals with severe acidity or gastritis

### Potential Interactions with Medications

* - May interact with diuretics and blood sugar medications

### Safe Dosage Guidelines

Always consult an Ayurvedic practitioner before using Chirchita extensively.

## Scientific Research on Chirchita

Recent studies validate Chirchita’s antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

### Nutritional Composition of Chirchita

Rich in alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, contributing to its medicinal benefits.

### Comparison with Similar Medicinal Plants

While both are blood purifiers, Chirchita is also a diuretic and digestive stimulant.

#### Unique Features of Apamarga

Unlike other herbs, Chirchita is beneficial for kidney health and weight management.

## Chirchita in Modern Herbal Medicine

Leading Ayurvedic brands include Chirchita in herbal formulations for digestion, detoxification, and immunity.

### Growing Trend of Chirchita in Natural Health Products

With increasing awareness of Ayurvedic remedies, Chirchita is gaining popularity in herbal teas, supplements, and skincare products.

## FAQs About Chirchita and Apamarga Plant

#### Q: Can Chirchita help with kidney stones?

A: Yes, its diuretic properties help flush out kidney stones naturally.

#### Q: Is Chirchita safe for daily use?

A: When taken in moderate amounts, it is safe. Always consult a healthcare provider.

## Conclusion

Chirchita, also called Apamarga, is an extremely potent Ayurvedic herb that is helpful for digestion, skin care, and kidney health. It can be added to both traditional and modern medicine to add value to daily wellness practices. Try adding Chirchita to your life through herbal teas, powders, or topical applications for a holistic approach to health.

One of the most medicinal plants mentioned in the Charaka Saṁhitā and Sushruda Saṁhitā is chirchita, which can bring genuine healing to a number of conditions in human beings. It has numerous healing properties which have proven to be beneficial since a long time. 

Dosha Balancing Properties of Chirchita: Vata, Pitta, and Kapha

* - Balances Vata and Kapha doshas
* - Helps in Pitta disorders when used in moderation

## Chirchita Plant Benefits

* - Anti-inflammatory
* - Diuretic
* - Digestive stimulant
* - Blood purifier
* - Antimicrobial

## Health Benefits of Chirchita

#### 1. Digestive Health
 <image:4958> 
Chirchita is also known to digest the food easily as it induces appetite, prevents bloating, and treats constipation.

#### 2. Skin Care

Since Chirchita contains antimicrobial properties, it is also used in the treatment of infections on the skin, wounds, and acne.

#### 3. Kidney and Urinary Tract Health

Apamarga is used as a diuretic to cleanse the kidneys and avoid infection of the urinary tract.

#### 4. Blood Purification

It purifies the blood and cleanses the skin by removing the toxins.

#### 5. Role in Weight Management

Chirchita increases the metabolism and facilitates the loss of fats, hence useful in managing weight.

### Other Therapeutic Uses

* - Relieves joint pain and inflammation
* - Treats respiratory issues like asthma and cough

## How to Use Chirchita (Apamarga Plant)

### Forms: Powder, Juice, Decoction, and Oil

* - Powder: Used for digestion and detoxification
* - Juice: Beneficial for skin and kidney health
* - Decoction: Helps with respiratory and urinary issues
* - Oil: Applied externally for wounds and joint pain

### Recipes: DIY Remedies Using Chirchita

1. - For Digestive Health: Mix 1 tsp Chirchita powder with warm water and honey.
2. - For Skin Issues: Apply Chirchita paste on acne and wounds.
3. - For Kidney Health: Drink Chirchita decoction once daily.

### Dosage Recommendations

* - Powder: 1–3 grams per day
* - Juice: 10–20 ml per day
* - Decoction: 30–50 ml per day

## Apamarga Plant Benefits in Ayurveda

The anti-inflammatory properties help to lessen swelling and pain.

### Benefits for Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
 <image:4956> 
Chirchita helps regulate blood sugar levels, making it useful for diabetics.Chirchita helps in curing the metabolic disorders and regulating blood sugar levels, Thus it is considered useful for the purpose of diabetes.

### Wound Healing and Antimicrobial Properties

It Improve wound healing and protects against bacterial infection. Side Effects and Precautions

## Side Effects and Precautions

### Who Should Avoid Chirchita?

* - Pregnant and breastfeeding women
* - Individuals with severe acidity or gastritis

### Potential Interactions with Medications

* - May interact with diuretics and blood sugar medications

### Safe Dosage Guidelines

Always consult an Ayurvedic practitioner before using Chirchita extensively.

## Scientific Research on Chirchita

Recent studies validate Chirchita’s antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

### Nutritional Composition of Chirchita

Rich in alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, contributing to its medicinal benefits.

### Comparison with Similar Medicinal Plants

While both are blood purifiers, Chirchita is also a diuretic and digestive stimulant.

#### Unique Features of Apamarga

Unlike other herbs, Chirchita is beneficial for kidney health and weight management.

## Chirchita in Modern Herbal Medicine

Leading Ayurvedic brands include Chirchita in herbal formulations for digestion, detoxification, and immunity.

### Growing Trend of Chirchita in Natural Health Products

With increasing awareness of Ayurvedic remedies, Chirchita is gaining popularity in herbal teas, supplements, and skincare products.

## FAQs About Chirchita and Apamarga Plant

#### Q: Can Chirchita help with kidney stones?

A: Yes, its diuretic properties help flush out kidney stones naturally.

#### Q: Is Chirchita safe for daily use?

A: When taken in moderate amounts, it is safe. Always consult a healthcare provider.

## Conclusion

Chirchita, also called Apamarga, is an extremely potent Ayurvedic herb that is helpful for digestion, skin care, and kidney health. It can be added to both traditional and modern medicine to add value to daily wellness practices. Try adding Chirchita to your life through herbal teas, powders, or topical applications for a holistic approach to health.

You are a web-search planner.
Given the task and prior session context, propose next search queries.
Return strict JSON with keys:
- "queries": array of strings
- "done": boolean

Guidelines:
- Keep queries specific and evidence-seeking.
- If enough evidence is already gathered, set done=true and queries=[].
- If you want to gather more information, set done=false and propose up to 5 new queries.
- You have multiple iterations to gather information, so you can search for facts building on previous findings.
- Use prior session context to answer follow-up questions efficiently and avoid repeating work.

Task:
Search for this claim: 'Apamarga root extract dissolves kidney stones of any size within 10-15 days' and find when/where this claim was first published. Look for scientific studies on Apamarga (Achyranthes aspera) and kidney stones, credible medical sources that support or refute this claim, and identify who is spreading this claim online. Also search for similar claims about Apamarga root and kidney stone treatment.

Prior session context:
Accepted evidence:
- Source: https://www.thip.media/health-news-fact-check/fact-check-can-apamarga-root-extract-effectively-dissolve-kidney-stones/132405/
  Summary: - An Instagram post by user ‘abhi.healthguru’ claims that consuming Apamarga root extract on an empty stomach dissolves kidney stones of any size.
- The source claim asserts that smaller kidney stones show effects in 2-3 days while larger stones clear up in 10-15 days.
- The Instagram post provides instructions to take 5-6 roots of Apamarga, crush them, and mix them in a glass of water for use.
- The Healthy Indian Project rates the claim that Apamarga root extract dissolves kidney stones in 10-15 days as ‘Mostly False’.
- Medical facts state that claims of kidney stones resolving on their own in a few days are unsupported by scientific data.
- Symptomatic stones ranging from 5 to 10 mm typically require cautious treatment lasting a few weeks to a month. <image:4952>  <image:4950>  <image:4951> - Source: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/f09c/be558349f185a609d299724b878b058b61e9.pdf
  Summary: - Achyranthes aspera is known as apamarga and belongs to the family Amaranthaceae.
- Apamarga is widely used in Ayurvedic medicine for ages as an herbal drug for kidney stones.
- The plant is an active component and typical ingredient of drug formulations such as cystone used to treat kidney stones.
- Scientific reports indicate the plant has anti-nephrolithiasis potency and ability to preserve renal functioning.
- Achyranthes aspera contains triterpenoid saponins possessing oleanolic acid and alkaloids like acanthine and betaine.
- Cystone is an Ayurvedic product by Himalaya Herbal that contains Achyranthes aspera for the management of kidney stones.
- Cystone elements act on all types of stones, helping to dissolve the stones and flush out the stone gravel through urination.
- Source: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/385866268_Traditional_Plants_Based_Treatment_for_Kidney_Stones_Insight_into_Herbal_Drugs
  Summary: ... kidney stones has a long and varied history. The use of natural remedies seldom claims to treat or dissolve the kidney stones themselves.
- Source: https://patents.google.com/patent/WO2012127498A9/en
  Summary: - The document is a WIPO patent publication (WO2012127498A9) titled 'A novel herbal composition for the treatment of kidney stone and other urinary tract disorders'.
- The patent inventor is Suresh Balkrishna PATANKAR.
- The herbal composition includes 'Apamargkshar', which is described as an extract from the flower, leaves, seed, root, and fruits of Achyranthus aspera (Apamarg/Aghada).
- The patent claims the composition is useful for the treatment of kidney stone and other urinary tract disorders.
- Clinical studies described in the patent report an 'Average duration of clearance' of 8.7 weeks for the 'Herbmed Plus' group.
- Case studies within the document describe treatment periods of 'one month' resulting in reduced surface area of calculi.
- The specified range for Apamargkshar in the composition is listed as 15-85%. <image:4945>  <image:4946>  <image:4948>  <image:4947> - Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22439435/
  Summary: - The webpage displays a PubMed record for a study titled Preventive and curative effects of Achyranthes aspera Linn. extract in experimentally induced nephrolithiasis.
- The article was published in the Indian Journal of Experimental Biology in March 2012.
- The study evaluated the efficacy of Achyranthes aspera in preventing and reducing the growth of calcium oxalate stones in an experimentally induced nephrolithiatic model.
- The research was conducted using rats where hyperoxaluria was induced using ethylene glycol and ammonium chloride.
- Aqueous extract of A. aspera was administered at doses of 500 and 1000 mg/kg body weight during the study.
- Treatment with A. aspera extract normalized levels of renal injury markers including lactate dehydrogenase and alkaline phosphatase.
- The study results indicated that Achyranthes aspera had an ability to maintain renal functioning and reduced renal injury.
- Source: https://japsonline.com/abstract.php?article_id=4032&sts=2
  Summary: The present work revealed that the saponin-rich segment of the methanolic extract of A. aspera efficiently dissolves CaOx renal stones.
- Source: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0975947617304084
  Summary: - The article describes a 28-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial evaluating a herbal formulation for renal calculi.
- The investigational product Subap Plus contains *Achyranthes aspera* Linn along with *Crataeva Nurvala*, *Musa paradisiaca*, and *Hordeum Vulgare*.
- Patients enrolled in the trial had asymptomatic renal calculi ranging in size from 4 to 9 mm.
- Extract of *Achyranthes aspera* has shown effective inhibitory action on CaOx nucleation and growth in vitro.
- The study results reported a significant reduction in the surface area and density of stones in the treatment group compared to the placebo group.
- A total of 84 patients were randomized in the study, with 34 in the treatment group and 31 in the placebo group completing the trial.
- The herbal formulation is proposed to act on the phases of stone formation including nucleation, growth, aggregation, and crystal retention. <image:4954>  <image:4953>  <image:4955>  <image:4953> - Source: https://www.pnrjournal.com/index.php/home/article/download/3868/4020/4811
  Summary: Researchers are identified evidence and research, in validating efficacy and safety ... www.health.nytimes.com/Kidney Stones Affecting More People, Earlier by ...
- Source: https://trial.medpath.com/clinical-trial/6fe37680429170ce/mta5nte0-arka-paneeya-kshara-apamarga-paneeya-kshara-mutrashmari-urolithiasis
  Summary: ... Apamarga paneeya kshara orally with sukoshna jala. assesment of patient will be done before the treatment and after treatment (28th day).
- Source: https://www.facebook.com/cnnnews18/posts/consuming-just-5-to-10-grams-of-apamarg-throughout-the-day-can-eliminate-stones-/1246943464141961/
  Summary: Consuming just 5 to 10 grams of Apamarg throughout the day can eliminate stones up to 20 to 25 mm in size. The key is to consume it ...
- Source: https://www.instagram.com/p/DOvfYLlE3_g/
  Summary: - The webpage features an Instagram video by the author @PRARBDHA AYURVED (vd Siddharth Kumar) posted on 2025-09-18.
- Apamarga (Achyranthes aspera) is traditionally used to treat kidney stones according to the provided text.
- Apamarga is described as a natural diuretic that helps flush toxins from the kidneys and may assist in breaking down kidney stones.
- A decoction made from the Apamarga plant can be used specifically for kidney stones.
- The plant should be used under medical supervision, especially for conditions like kidney stones.
- The plant is not recommended for use by pregnant or nursing women. <video:497> - Source: https://www.baidyanathayurved.com/blogs/blogs/chirchita-apamarg-prickly-chaff-ayurvedic-benefits-uses-and-side-effects?srsltid=AfmBOorv-4COQoN81fRjDSRfPRNRfYz8qnalo5yOo5GQevoQiCyuqPiY

### About Journal

Research Journal of Pharmacy and Technology (RJPT) is an international, peer-reviewed, multidisciplinary journal, devoted to pharmaceutical sciences. The aim of RJPT is to increase the impact of pharmaceutical research both in academia and industry, with strong emphasis on quality and originality. RJPT publishes Original Research Articles, Short Communications, Review Articles in all areas of pharmaceutical sciences from the discovery of a drug up to clinical evaluation. Topics covered are: Pharmaceutics and Pharmacokinetics; Pharmaceutical chemistry including medicinal and analytical chemistry; Pharmacognosy including herbal products standardization and Phytochemistry; Pharmacology: Allied sciences including drug regulatory affairs, Pharmaceutical Marketing, Pharmaceutical Microbiology, Pharmaceutical biochemistry, Pharmaceutical Education and Hospital Pharmacy.

### About Journal

Research Journal of Pharmacy and Technology (RJPT) is an international, peer-reviewed, multidisciplinary journal, devoted to pharmaceutical sciences. The aim of RJPT is to increase the impact of pharmaceutical research both in academia and industry, with strong emphasis on quality and originality. RJPT publishes Original Research Articles, Short Communications, Review Articles in all areas of pharmaceutical sciences from the discovery of a drug up to clinical evaluation. Topics covered are: Pharmaceutics and Pharmacokinetics; Pharmaceutical chemistry including medicinal and analytical chemistry; Pharmacognosy including herbal products standardization and Phytochemistry; Pharmacology: Allied sciences including drug regulatory affairs, Pharmaceutical Marketing, Pharmaceutical Microbiology, Pharmaceutical biochemistry, Pharmaceutical Education and Hospital Pharmacy.

## Abstract

Urolithiasis, a disease of kidney stones, is a prevalent and recurrent condition characterized by the formation of hard mineral deposits in the urinary tract, leading to significant morbidity and a substantial healthcare burden worldwide. With recurrence rates ranging from 30 to 50% and a lifetime prevalence of 9–12% in industrialized populations, effective prevention and management strategies are crucial. Traditional approaches to urolithiasis, including pharmacological agents and surgical interventions, emphasize medical interventions and lifestyle changes; however, the persistent recurrence and impact on quality of life underscore the necessity for more comprehensive solutions. Recent advancements have focused on the potential of nutraceuticals, including herbal extracts, vitamins, and minerals, as adjuncts in the prevention and treatment of kidney stones. These agents may exert beneficial effects by inhibiting stone crystal formation, modulating urinary pH, reducing urinary calcium and oxalate excretion, enhancing diuresis, and supporting overall renal function. Moreover, these plant-based therapies may help alleviate symptoms such as pain and inflammation related to stone episodes. This systematic review synthesizes findings from 14 randomized controlled trials to assess the therapeutic impact and safety profile of various herbal interventions in patients with diagnosed kidney or ureteral stones. Thus, evidence from these recent clinical trials has increasingly supported the efficacy of single-herb and polyherbal formulations, such as *Nigella sativa*, *Alhagi maurorum*, Subap Plus, and Palasha Kshara, in modulating urinary risk factors and promoting stone expulsion. For instance, these trials demonstrated promising outcomes for several herbs: *Portulaca oleracea* significantly increased urinary citrate and reduced calcium, Traditional Persian Medicine-based Mofatet powder markedly reduced stone size and improved urinary parameters, *Alhagi maurorum* distillates and extracts showed comparable efficacy to hydrochlorothiazide in stone expulsion, and *Nigella sativa* performed as well as or better than tamsulosin in both pain relief and stone passage. Additional polyherbal remedies, such as Subap Plus, Palasha Kshara, and BNO 1040 extract, also yielded favourable results in improving stone clearance, easing symptoms, and potentially preventing recurrence. Notably, most studies reported good short-term tolerability and minimal adverse effects. Despite these promising findings, limitations in sample size, standardization of formulations, lack of comprehensive adverse events reporting, and need for long-term follow-up highlight the necessity for further rigorous trials.

**Keywords:** Urolithiasis, Kidney stones, Nutraceuticals, Recurrence prevention, Urinary health

## Introduction

Kidney stone disease (KSD), also known as nephrolithiasis or urolithiasis, refers to the formation of solid crystalline deposits within the kidneys or urinary tract (Xu et al. [2023](#CR153)). These stones arise from the supersaturation and precipitation of urinary solutes, typically under conditions of altered pH, dehydration, or metabolic imbalance. While often presenting with acute pain, the clinical spectrum ranges from incidental findings to recurrent, debilitating episodes that can lead to renal damage (Uribarri [2020](#CR144); Hoffman et al. [2021](#CR65)).

Kidney stones are heterogeneous in both composition and etiology and are generally classified based on their dominant chemical constituents (Pak et al. [2003](#CR98)). The most common type is calcium oxalate, accounting for approximately 70–80% of all stones (Coe et al. [1992](#CR26); Pak et al. [2003](#CR98); Taylor et al. [2005](#CR141)). These may form independently or in combination with calcium phosphate and are often associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, or hypocitraturia (Cupisti et al. [2007](#CR29); Kustov et al. [2016](#CR78)). Uric acid stones, comprising 5–10% of cases, are radiolucent and typically form in acidic urine, particularly in patients with metabolic syndrome, gout, or high-purine diets. Their formation is strongly influenced by low urinary pH, rather than systemic uric acid levels alone (Tasian and Copelovitch [2014](#CR138); Nevo et al. [2020](#CR94)). Struvite stones, also known as infection stones, are composed of magnesium ammonium phosphate and linked to chronic urinary tract infections by urease-producing bacteria (Das et al. [2017](#CR32)). Cystine stones are rare and result from a genetic defect in amino acid reabsorption, leading to excess urinary cystine. They tend to recur and are often difficult to manage, requiring a combination of hydration, alkalinization, and pharmacological therapy (D’Ambrosio et al. [2022](#CR31)). Other less common types include xanthine, drug-induced stones (e.g. indinavir), and mixed-composition stones, which reflect metabolic or environmental factors (Seegmiller [1968](#CR122); Daudon and Jungers [2004](#CR33)). Nephrolithiasis, the formation of stones within the renal collecting system, affects approximately 10% of the population over their lifetime and is associated with significant healthcare costs and patient burden (Chewcharat and Curhan [2021](#CR25)). Pathogenesis involves the crystallization of urinary solutes due to supersaturation and impaired inhibitory mechanisms. Despite advances in surgical treatment, recurrence remains a significant concern, shifting the focus toward prevention by elucidating the underlying mechanisms and risk profiles. The aim of the present review is to provide an important assessment of the role of dietary plants as natural supplements in the prevention or management of kidney stones and indicate underlying pharmacological mechanisms.

### Prevalence and demographics

KSD has evolved from an episodic, localized ailment into a pervasive global health challenge, driven by changes in lifestyle, environment, and diagnostic awareness. In the USA, long-term data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) illustrate this shift: early survey cycles relied on a simple “Have you ever passed a stone?” question to capture symptomatic cases, yielding a prevalence of 3.8% between 1976 and 1994. Later NHANES iterations questioned the number of stones passed, distinguishing single events from recurrent or silent (asymptomatic) stones. These refinements coincided with a rise to 5.2% by the mid-1990s and, more recently, to approximately 9.3% between 2007 and 2016 (Stamatelou et al. [2003](#CR135); Romero et al. [2010](#CR113); Hyams and Matlaga [2014](#CR67)).

A parallel surge has occurred in Asia’s so-called “stone belt” countries. In China, prevalence climbed steadily after 1978, reaching 5.8% by 2013; in Japan, it roughly doubled to 9% by 2005; South Korea saw rates jump from 3.5% in 1998 to 11.5% in 2013; southern Iran now reports over 21%; and Saudi Arabia experienced a sharp rise from 6.8 to 19.1% over a comparable period. Yet, these figures derive from heterogeneous case-finding methods, self-reported histories prone to recall bias, imaging studies of varying sensitivity, and medical records-based reviews that may overlook silent stones, making direct cross-country comparisons problematic and obscuring the relative impact of dietary, metabolic, genetic, and climatic drivers (Nelli [2004](#CR93); Edvardsson et al. [2013](#CR41), [2018](#CR42); Liu et al. [2018](#CR84)).

Standardizing diagnostics, including imaging thresholds, stone composition, and symptoms, as well as unifying surveillance protocols, are essential for accurately mapping global KSD burden. Harmonized data will reveal regional risk factors and guide targeted prevention, either through hydration education, sodium restriction policies, or heat-dehydration countermeasures, to curb the rising incidence worldwide.

### Health implications

While often perceived as an isolated urological event, KSD carries broad and lasting health implications. The acute phase, marked by severe pain, hematuria, and risk of obstruction, can lead to emergency visits, hospitalizations, and surgical intervention. However, the burden extends well beyond acute management.

Recurrent stone formation is common. Studies indicate that approximately 50% of individuals experience a recurrence within 5 to 10 years after their first kidney stone episode (Shastri et al. [2023](#CR128)). This high relapse rate underscores the need for ongoing preventive strategies.

Emerging evidence suggests that KSD is associated with an increased risk of developing CKD. Stone formers, particularly those with recurrent episodes, are at higher risk for sustained reductions in glomerular filtration rate and elevated serum creatinine levels (Rule et al. [2009](#CR114)). The mechanisms may involve obstructive uropathy, recurrent infections, and direct renal parenchymal injury.

KSD often coexists with systemic metabolic conditions. Obesity, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome are not only risk factors for stone formation but may also be exacerbated by it (Rahman et al. [2021](#CR106)). The interplay between these conditions complicates management and increases long-term morbidity.

Beyond physical health, KSD can significantly influence mental well-being. Recurrent pain episodes, dietary restrictions, and anxiety over potential recurrences contribute to psychological distress, including depression and decreased quality of life (Patel et al. [2017](#CR102)). These effects are particularly pronounced in younger patients and those with frequent stone episodes.

Given its increasing prevalence and systemic associations, KSD should be regarded not merely as a localized urological condition but as a chronic, relapsing disorder with multisystem implications. This perspective necessitates proactive, long-term management strategies that address both the physical and psychosocial dimensions of the disease.

## Abstract

Urolithiasis, a disease of kidney stones, is a prevalent and recurrent condition characterized by the formation of hard mineral deposits in the urinary tract, leading to significant morbidity and a substantial healthcare burden worldwide. With recurrence rates ranging from 30 to 50% and a lifetime prevalence of 9–12% in industrialized populations, effective prevention and management strategies are crucial. Traditional approaches to urolithiasis, including pharmacological agents and surgical interventions, emphasize medical interventions and lifestyle changes; however, the persistent recurrence and impact on quality of life underscore the necessity for more comprehensive solutions. Recent advancements have focused on the potential of nutraceuticals, including herbal extracts, vitamins, and minerals, as adjuncts in the prevention and treatment of kidney stones. These agents may exert beneficial effects by inhibiting stone crystal formation, modulating urinary pH, reducing urinary calcium and oxalate excretion, enhancing diuresis, and supporting overall renal function. Moreover, these plant-based therapies may help alleviate symptoms such as pain and inflammation related to stone episodes. This systematic review synthesizes findings from 14 randomized controlled trials to assess the therapeutic impact and safety profile of various herbal interventions in patients with diagnosed kidney or ureteral stones. Thus, evidence from these recent clinical trials has increasingly supported the efficacy of single-herb and polyherbal formulations, such as *Nigella sativa*, *Alhagi maurorum*, Subap Plus, and Palasha Kshara, in modulating urinary risk factors and promoting stone expulsion. For instance, these trials demonstrated promising outcomes for several herbs: *Portulaca oleracea* significantly increased urinary citrate and reduced calcium, Traditional Persian Medicine-based Mofatet powder markedly reduced stone size and improved urinary parameters, *Alhagi maurorum* distillates and extracts showed comparable efficacy to hydrochlorothiazide in stone expulsion, and *Nigella sativa* performed as well as or better than tamsulosin in both pain relief and stone passage. Additional polyherbal remedies, such as Subap Plus, Palasha Kshara, and BNO 1040 extract, also yielded favourable results in improving stone clearance, easing symptoms, and potentially preventing recurrence. Notably, most studies reported good short-term tolerability and minimal adverse effects. Despite these promising findings, limitations in sample size, standardization of formulations, lack of comprehensive adverse events reporting, and need for long-term follow-up highlight the necessity for further rigorous trials.

**Keywords:** Urolithiasis, Kidney stones, Nutraceuticals, Recurrence prevention, Urinary health

## Introduction

Kidney stone disease (KSD), also known as nephrolithiasis or urolithiasis, refers to the formation of solid crystalline deposits within the kidneys or urinary tract (Xu et al. [2023](#CR153)). These stones arise from the supersaturation and precipitation of urinary solutes, typically under conditions of altered pH, dehydration, or metabolic imbalance. While often presenting with acute pain, the clinical spectrum ranges from incidental findings to recurrent, debilitating episodes that can lead to renal damage (Uribarri [2020](#CR144); Hoffman et al. [2021](#CR65)).

Kidney stones are heterogeneous in both composition and etiology and are generally classified based on their dominant chemical constituents (Pak et al. [2003](#CR98)). The most common type is calcium oxalate, accounting for approximately 70–80% of all stones (Coe et al. [1992](#CR26); Pak et al. [2003](#CR98); Taylor et al. [2005](#CR141)). These may form independently or in combination with calcium phosphate and are often associated with hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, or hypocitraturia (Cupisti et al. [2007](#CR29); Kustov et al. [2016](#CR78)). Uric acid stones, comprising 5–10% of cases, are radiolucent and typically form in acidic urine, particularly in patients with metabolic syndrome, gout, or high-purine diets. Their formation is strongly influenced by low urinary pH, rather than systemic uric acid levels alone (Tasian and Copelovitch [2014](#CR138); Nevo et al. [2020](#CR94)). Struvite stones, also known as infection stones, are composed of magnesium ammonium phosphate and linked to chronic urinary tract infections by urease-producing bacteria (Das et al. [2017](#CR32)). Cystine stones are rare and result from a genetic defect in amino acid reabsorption, leading to excess urinary cystine. They tend to recur and are often difficult to manage, requiring a combination of hydration, alkalinization, and pharmacological therapy (D’Ambrosio et al. [2022](#CR31)). Other less common types include xanthine, drug-induced stones (e.g. indinavir), and mixed-composition stones, which reflect metabolic or environmental factors (Seegmiller [1968](#CR122); Daudon and Jungers [2004](#CR33)). Nephrolithiasis, the formation of stones within the renal collecting system, affects approximately 10% of the population over their lifetime and is associated with significant healthcare costs and patient burden (Chewcharat and Curhan [2021](#CR25)). Pathogenesis involves the crystallization of urinary solutes due to supersaturation and impaired inhibitory mechanisms. Despite advances in surgical treatment, recurrence remains a significant concern, shifting the focus toward prevention by elucidating the underlying mechanisms and risk profiles. The aim of the present review is to provide an important assessment of the role of dietary plants as natural supplements in the prevention or management of kidney stones and indicate underlying pharmacological mechanisms.

### Prevalence and demographics

KSD has evolved from an episodic, localized ailment into a pervasive global health challenge, driven by changes in lifestyle, environment, and diagnostic awareness. In the USA, long-term data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) illustrate this shift: early survey cycles relied on a simple “Have you ever passed a stone?” question to capture symptomatic cases, yielding a prevalence of 3.8% between 1976 and 1994. Later NHANES iterations questioned the number of stones passed, distinguishing single events from recurrent or silent (asymptomatic) stones. These refinements coincided with a rise to 5.2% by the mid-1990s and, more recently, to approximately 9.3% between 2007 and 2016 (Stamatelou et al. [2003](#CR135); Romero et al. [2010](#CR113); Hyams and Matlaga [2014](#CR67)).

A parallel surge has occurred in Asia’s so-called “stone belt” countries. In China, prevalence climbed steadily after 1978, reaching 5.8% by 2013; in Japan, it roughly doubled to 9% by 2005; South Korea saw rates jump from 3.5% in 1998 to 11.5% in 2013; southern Iran now reports over 21%; and Saudi Arabia experienced a sharp rise from 6.8 to 19.1% over a comparable period. Yet, these figures derive from heterogeneous case-finding methods, self-reported histories prone to recall bias, imaging studies of varying sensitivity, and medical records-based reviews that may overlook silent stones, making direct cross-country comparisons problematic and obscuring the relative impact of dietary, metabolic, genetic, and climatic drivers (Nelli [2004](#CR93); Edvardsson et al. [2013](#CR41), [2018](#CR42); Liu et al. [2018](#CR84)).

Standardizing diagnostics, including imaging thresholds, stone composition, and symptoms, as well as unifying surveillance protocols, are essential for accurately mapping global KSD burden. Harmonized data will reveal regional risk factors and guide targeted prevention, either through hydration education, sodium restriction policies, or heat-dehydration countermeasures, to curb the rising incidence worldwide.

### Health implications

While often perceived as an isolated urological event, KSD carries broad and lasting health implications. The acute phase, marked by severe pain, hematuria, and risk of obstruction, can lead to emergency visits, hospitalizations, and surgical intervention. However, the burden extends well beyond acute management.

Recurrent stone formation is common. Studies indicate that approximately 50% of individuals experience a recurrence within 5 to 10 years after their first kidney stone episode (Shastri et al. [2023](#CR128)). This high relapse rate underscores the need for ongoing preventive strategies.

Emerging evidence suggests that KSD is associated with an increased risk of developing CKD. Stone formers, particularly those with recurrent episodes, are at higher risk for sustained reductions in glomerular filtration rate and elevated serum creatinine levels (Rule et al. [2009](#CR114)). The mechanisms may involve obstructive uropathy, recurrent infections, and direct renal parenchymal injury.

KSD often coexists with systemic metabolic conditions. Obesity, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome are not only risk factors for stone formation but may also be exacerbated by it (Rahman et al. [2021](#CR106)). The interplay between these conditions complicates management and increases long-term morbidity.

Beyond physical health, KSD can significantly influence mental well-being. Recurrent pain episodes, dietary restrictions, and anxiety over potential recurrences contribute to psychological distress, including depression and decreased quality of life (Patel et al. [2017](#CR102)). These effects are particularly pronounced in younger patients and those with frequent stone episodes.

Given its increasing prevalence and systemic associations, KSD should be regarded not merely as a localized urological condition but as a chronic, relapsing disorder with multisystem implications. This perspective necessitates proactive, long-term management strategies that address both the physical and psychosocial dimensions of the disease.

takeaways.text:
- The provided text refers to the plant *Achyranthes aspera* (scientific name for Apamarga) rather than using the common name Apamarga explicitly.
- *Achyranthes aspera* is listed as a component of the Subap Plus herbal formulation, which also includes *Crateva nurvala*, *Musa x paradisiaca*, and *Hordeum vulgare*.
- The clinical trial evaluating Subap Plus (Patankar et al. 2020) had an intervention duration of 24 weeks (6 months).
- In the Subap Plus trial, significant reductions in stone surface area and density were observed, along with a higher stone expulsion rate compared to placebo.
- *Achyranthes aspera* is also identified as one of 13 plant ingredients in the Ashmarihara Kwatha decoction used in a trial with an 8-week duration.
- The review mentions that *Alhagi maurorum* distillate significantly reduced stone removal time by approximately 4 days in patients with ureteral stones.
- The systematic review synthesized findings from 14 randomized controlled trials assessing herbal interventions for urolithiasis.
